questionText: A parabola has Cartesian equation $y^{2} = 8x$. Find parametric equations for the parabola in terms of a parameter $t$, state the range of $t$ needed to describe the whole curve, and state the range of $x$.

steps.description: Compare with the standard parabola form
steps.explanation: A right-opening parabola $y^{2} = 4ax$ has a known parametric form, so we first read off $a$ by matching $4a$ with the given coefficient $8$.

steps.workingLatex: x = a t^{2}, \qquad y = 2 a t
steps.explanation: For $y^{2} = 4ax$ the standard parameter satisfies these two equations; substituting them makes the Cartesian equation an identity, which we will confirm.
Putting $a = 2$ into the standard form gives concrete parametric equations for this particular parabola.

steps.description: Compute each side of the Cartesian equation
steps.explanation: Both sides equal $16t^{2}$ for every $t$, which shows the point always lies on $y^{2} = 8x$.
Since the two expressions are equal for all $t$, the parametrisation genuinely traces the parabola.
Nothing stops $t$ taking any real value, and each real $t$ gives a distinct point, so all real $t$ are needed to sweep the whole curve.

steps.description: Reason about the range of x
steps.explanation: A square is never negative, so $2t^{2}$ cannot be negative; this tells us how far the curve reaches horizontally.
The parabola lies entirely in the region $x \ge 0$, opening to the right from its vertex at the origin.
These parametric equations, with $t$ over all reals, describe the entire parabola, which occupies $x \ge 0$.
